quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Trophic level"?

options.text: A 10-step metabolic pathway in the cytoplasm that converts glucose (6C) into 2 pyruvate (3C), producing 2 net ATP and 2 NADH
A feeding level in a food chain: producers (autotrophs), primary consumers (herbivores), secondary consumers (carnivores), decomposers
Double-membrane organelle where cellular respiration produces ATP; has own circular DNA, supporting the endosymbiotic theory
A nucleotide change that creates a premature stop codon, producing a truncated, usually nonfunctional protein

quiz_questions.explanation: "Trophic level" is defined as: A feeding level in a food chain: producers (autotrophs), primary consumers (herbivores), secondary consumers (carnivores), decomposers. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "10% rule"?

options.text: Small intracellular molecules (cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP₃) that relay and amplify signals from the receptor to target proteins
Direct transfer of a phosphate group from a substrate molecule to ADP to form ATP; occurs in glycolysis and the Krebs cycle
Only ~10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next; the rest is lost as heat through metabolic processes
A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid or stop signal; 64 total codons, 61 code for amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "10% rule" is defined as: Only ~10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next; the rest is lost as heat through metabolic processes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Net primary productivity (NPP)"?

options.text: The rate at which producers store energy as biomass minus their own respiratory losses; NPP = GPP - respiration
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase; the most abundant enzyme on Earth; fixes CO₂ but also has oxygenase activity (photorespiration)
A signaling molecule (hormone, neurotransmitter, growth factor) that binds specifically to a receptor protein
A simple sugar (glucose, fructose, galactose) with the general formula (CH₂O)n; the monomer of carbohydrates

quiz_questions.explanation: "Net primary productivity (NPP)" is defined as: The rate at which producers store energy as biomass minus their own respiratory losses; NPP = GPP - respiration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Biogeochemical cycle"?

options.text: Multiple genes contribute to a single trait, producing continuous variation (e.g., skin color, height, intelligence)
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase; the most abundant enzyme on Earth; fixes CO₂ but also has oxygenase activity (photorespiration)
The pathway by which a chemical element (C, N, P, H₂O) moves through the biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem
A gene encoding a protein that inhibits cell division (e.g., p53 triggers apoptosis in damaged cells, Rb blocks G1/S transition)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Biogeochemical cycle" is defined as: The pathway by which a chemical element (C, N, P, H₂O) moves through the biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nitrogen fixation"?

options.text: The percentage of offspring showing recombinant phenotypes; used to calculate the distance between linked genes
The conversion of atmospheric N₂ to biologically usable NH₃/NH₄⁺ by bacteria (Rhizobium) or lightning; the only natural source of usable nitrogen
The proportion of each genotype in a population: p² (AA) + 2pq (Aa) + q² (aa) = 1 under Hardy-Weinberg conditions
The molecular machine for translation; composed of large and small subunits containing rRNA and proteins; has A, P, and E sites

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nitrogen fixation" is defined as: The conversion of atmospheric N₂ to biologically usable NH₃/NH₄⁺ by bacteria (Rhizobium) or lightning; the only natural source of usable nitrogen. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A feeding level in a food chain: producers (autotrophs), primary consumers (herbivores), secondary consumers (carnivores), decomposers"?

options.text: Activation energy (Ea)
Glycolysis
Linked genes
Trophic level

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Trophic level".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Only ~10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next; the rest is lost as heat through metabolic processes"?

options.text: Recombination frequency
Krebs cycle (citric acid cycle)
10% rule
Hydrogen bond

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "10% rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The rate at which producers store energy as biomass minus their own respiratory losses; NPP = GPP - respiration"?

options.text: Crossing over
Net primary productivity (NPP)
Endomembrane system
r-selected species

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Net primary productivity (NPP)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The pathway by which a chemical element (C, N, P, H₂O) moves through the biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem"?

options.text: Density-dependent inhibition
Frameshift mutation
CRISPR-Cas9
Biogeochemical cycle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Biogeochemical cycle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The conversion of atmospheric N₂ to biologically usable NH₃/NH₄⁺ by bacteria (Rhizobium) or lightning; the only natural source of usable nitrogen"?

options.text: Genetic map
Leading strand
Molecular clock
Nitrogen fixation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nitrogen fixation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Ecosystem Ecology is accurate?

options.text: Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
Phospholipids form the basis of cell membrane bilayers due to their amphipathic nature (hydrophilic head, hydrophobic tails).
Water is split (photolysis) at PSII, releasing O₂.
Ecosystem Ecology studies energy flow and nutrient cycling through ecosystems.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ecosystem Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: Energy flows unidirectionally through trophic levels with ~10% efficiency (10% rule): producers → primary consumers → secondary consumers → tertiary consumers.
In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: 5' cap addition, 3' poly-A tail, and splicing to remove introns and join exons.
Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
Sexual selection and artificial selection are special cases of selection that shape traits beyond survival.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ecosystem Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: Enzymes are typically proteins with a specific active site that binds substrates via the induced fit model.
Nutrients (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water) cycle between biotic and abiotic components.
Triglycerides (fats and oils) store energy (9 kcal/g) through ester bonds between glycerol and three fatty acids.

Island biogeography theory (MacArthur and Wilson) predicts that species richness on an island reflects a balance between immigration and extinction rates, influenced by island size and distance from the mainland.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ecosystem Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: Competitive inhibitors resemble the substrate and compete for the active site, while noncompetitive inhibitors bind elsewhere and reduce catalytic activity.
The enzyme RuBisCO catalyzes carbon fixation (CO₂ + RuBP → 2 molecules of 3-PGA), making it the most abundant protein on Earth.
Net primary productivity (NPP) measures the rate of energy storage by producers and varies by biome, with tropical rainforests and estuaries among the most productive.
Biotechnology Applications encompasses the tools and techniques used to manipulate DNA.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ecosystem Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: Sexual selection and artificial selection are special cases of selection that shape traits beyond survival.
Ecosystem Ecology studies energy flow and nutrient cycling through ecosystems.
Current extinction rates are 100-1,000 times higher than background rates due to habitat destruction, invasive species, overexploitation, pollution, and climate change (HIPPO threats).
Repair mechanisms include base excision repair (BER) for damaged bases, nucleotide excision repair (NER) for bulky lesions, and mismatch repair for replication errors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ecosystem Ecology as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: RT-PCR diagnostic tests detecting viral RNA (COVID-19) by reverse-transcribing RNA to DNA and amplifying specific sequences
Biomagnification of mercury and DDT through food chains: top predators (eagles, tuna) accumulate the highest concentrations, explaining why large predatory fish have mercury advisories

Wolf reintroduction into Yellowstone (1995) demonstrating trophic cascades: wolves reduced elk overgrazing, allowing riparian vegetation recovery, which stabilized stream banks and increased beaver populations
Human population growth following a roughly exponential pattern from 1 billion (1800) to 8 billion (2022), with demographic transition slowing growth in developed nations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ecosystem Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: Cyanide poisoning blocking Complex IV of the ETC, preventing electron transfer to O₂ and halting all aerobic ATP production
Metabolic interconnections: Krebs cycle intermediates serve as precursors for amino acid synthesis, fatty acid synthesis, and gluconeogenesis
Carbon cycle disruption: fossil fuel combustion releasing 36+ billion tons of CO₂ annually, faster than natural sinks can absorb, driving climate change
Genetic rescue of the Florida panther: introducing 8 female Texas pumas in 1995 restored genetic diversity and tripled the population from ~25 to ~200 individuals

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ecosystem Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Ecosystem Ecology applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: DNA profiling (forensic fingerprinting) using short tandem repeat (STR) polymorphisms for criminal identification with error rates below 1 in a billion
Stem cell differentiation controlled by sequential gene regulation, with the same genome producing >200 different specialized cell types in the human body
Sustainable agriculture using nitrogen-fixing cover crops (legumes) to reduce dependence on synthetic nitrogen fertilizers that contribute to water pollution
α-amanitin (death cap mushroom toxin) inhibiting RNA polymerase II, blocking transcription and causing fatal liver failure within days

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ecosystem Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Ecosystem Ecology?

options.text: Biomagnification of mercury and DDT through food chains: top predators (eagles, tuna) accumulate the highest concentrations, explaining why large predatory fish have mercury advisories
Conservation biology using Hardy-Weinberg analysis to assess genetic health of endangered species populations and plan genetic rescue interventions
Genetic rescue of the Florida panther: introducing 8 female Texas pumas in 1995 restored genetic diversity and tripled the population from ~25 to ~200 individuals
Darwin's finches on the Galápagos Islands illustrating adaptive radiation: 14 species with different beak shapes evolved from a single ancestor to exploit different food sources

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ecosystem Ecology.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Ecosystem Ecology in practice?

options.text: Conservation biology using Hardy-Weinberg analysis to assess genetic health of endangered species populations and plan genetic rescue interventions
Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Cancer immunotherapy using checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4) to re-enable the immune system's ability to attack cancer cells
Carbon cycle disruption: fossil fuel combustion releasing 36+ billion tons of CO₂ annually, faster than natural sinks can absorb, driving climate change

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ecosystem Ecology.
